1: The milk/solid food developmental metaphor (nepiois en Christō, infants in Christ) reflects the widespread educational convention in the ancient world: philosophical schools distinguished between beginners receiving elementary instruction and advanced students ready for deeper teaching. Epictetus, Philo, and Plutarch all use the milk-solid food image for this pedagogical distinction. Paul applies it to the Corinthian community: their factional behavior demonstrates that despite the spiritual gifts they display, they are functioning as spiritually immature.
2: The dietary metaphor is carried through: gala (milk) for the immature, broma (solid food) for the mature. In Hellenistic Jewish wisdom literature (Philo, On Husbandry 9), the milk/food metaphor specifically contrasts elementary moral instruction with advanced philosophical wisdom. Paul's critique is pointed: the Corinthians boast of wisdom and spiritual gifts, yet their behavior shows they have not moved beyond the elementary stage of Christian formation.
3: Sarkinoi (fleshly, made of flesh): Paul's diagnosis of the factional behavior — zēlos (jealousy) and eris (strife) — as evidence of sarx (flesh-controlled) existence rather than pneumatic maturity. In Stoic ethics, faction and jealousy were paradigmatic passions (pathē) that indicated the absence of philosophical virtue. Paul uses similar categories but locates the cure not in philosophical training but in the Spirit's transformation of desire and community-orientation.
4: The names Paul-Apollos recur as the specific instance of the general problem. The Alexandrian philosophical tradition (Philo was the great example) combined Jewish scriptural learning with the highest Greek rhetorical and philosophical sophistication. Apollos brought this Alexandrian formation to Corinth and attracted followers among those who valued eloquence and philosophical depth. Paul's insistence on his own non-Sophistic style (1 Corinthians 2:1-5) was in direct contrast to Apollos's Alexandrian learning — creating the aesthetic-rhetorical division the factions exploited.
5: Diakonoi (servants/ministers): Paul deliberately downgrading himself and Apollos from the status of patron-figures to servants. In the Greco-Roman patronal system, followers attached themselves to powerful patrons for status and benefit; Paul insists that the apostles are not patrons but slaves (doulos, 1 Corinthians 3:5) through whom God acts. The emphasis on God as the one who gives growth is anti-patronal: the community owes its existence to God, not to the rhetorical charisma of its founders.
6: The agricultural metaphor — planting and watering — describes the sequential labor of different apostles in the same field. Paul founded the Corinthian community (Acts 18:1-17); Apollos subsequently taught and deepened the community (Acts 18:27-28). The division between their followers misunderstands the relationship: they are not competing farmers but co-laborers in the same field. Ho de Theos ēuxanen (God was causing the growth): imperfect tense — God's growth-giving was continuous throughout both Paul's and Apollos's work.
7: The plant metaphor makes the point starkly: the planter and waterer are ouden (nothing) in relation to the growth; only God, who gives the growth, is significant. This is a deliberate assault on the patron-client mechanism driving the factions: if the apostles are nothing in themselves, attaching one's identity to them is attaching it to nothing.
8: Each will receive their reward (misthos) according to their own labor: Paul uses the agricultural wage-laborer context. The eschatological judgment will evaluate the quality of the building work (1 Corinthians 3:10-15), not the worker's rhetorical prestige. This reframes apostolic competition away from crowd-approval and toward divine evaluation — a complete inversion of the Sophistic prestige-economy.
9: Theou georgion, Theou oikodomē este (you are God's field, God's building): the metaphor shifts from agriculture to architecture, anticipating the temple-building language of 1 Corinthians 3:16-17. In Roman urbanism, a significant public building project was among the most visible displays of patronal power and civic benefaction — the building inscription naming the patron was a permanent statement of status. Paul applies the building metaphor to the community but removes the patron's name: it is God's building, not Paul's or Apollos's.
10: Paul as sophos architektōn (skilled master-builder): the architektōn in Roman building practice was the one who designed and supervised construction, not the one who laid individual stones. Paul's apostolic role is the founding design; others build upon it. The Isthmian Games at Corinth required major construction projects; the Corinthians would be familiar with large-scale building and its demands for a single reliable foundation.
11: Themelion gar allon oudeis dynatai theinai para ton keimenon, hos estin Iesous Christos (no one can lay a foundation other than the one already laid, which is Jesus Christ): the metaphor has become theological claim. In Roman construction, a building was only as reliable as its foundation — on uneven rock, no superstructure was stable. Paul's claim: the only reliable theological foundation is Christ himself, not any apostolic personality or doctrinal innovation. The Corinthian factions are building superstructures on the wrong ground.
12: The building materials — gold, silver, precious stones (durable), versus wood, hay, straw (combustible) — reflect actual Roman construction practices: the wealthiest buildings used marble, granite, and costly stone; the poor built with timber and thatch. The metaphor evaluates the quality of community-building work: some contributions will endure eschatological testing, others will not. The quality is evaluated at the eschatological fire, not by contemporary prestige or popularity.
13: Hē hēmera dēlōsei (the Day will reveal it): the Day of the Lord as the ultimate quality-evaluator. En pyri apokalyptetai (revealed by fire): fire as divine judgment is a consistent prophetic image (Amos 7:4; Isaiah 66:15-16; Malachi 3:2). The metaphor of fire testing construction materials is from everyday experience: a fire that destroys a building reveals what was genuinely durable and what was not. The eschatological fire will similarly reveal the lasting quality of apostolic and community-building work.
14: The builder whose work survives the eschatological fire receives a misthos (reward/wage): Paul maintains the eschatological accountability of apostolic labor. This is not works-based salvation (1 Corinthians 3:10-15 address builders, not building-residents) but apostolic stewardship: those who build the community of God's people will be evaluated on the quality of their work.
15: Hōs dia pyros (as though through fire): the image of barely surviving a house fire — escaping with one's life but losing everything accumulated. Paul is not describing punishment but loss: the builder whose work is burned survives (sōthēsetai) but has nothing to show. The rabbinic tradition spoke similarly of those who would inherit the world to come but with nothing to bring with them. This is pastoral warning to the apostles about the quality of their community-building work.
16: The community as naos Theou (God's temple): Paul makes explicit what the agricultural and architectural metaphors were approaching. In Jewish thought, the Jerusalem Temple was the dwelling-place of the divine Shekhinah (presence); for the Second Temple period, the Qumran community understood itself as a living temple constituted by human beings living as God's dwelling. Paul shares this view: the gathered community is the naos — the inner sanctuary, not merely the outer court. The Spirit's indwelling (oikei, present tense: is continually dwelling) constitutes the community as sacred space.
17: Ei tis ton naon tou Theou phtheirei, phtherei touton ho Theos (if anyone destroys God's temple, God will destroy that person): the temple-protection principle applied to the community. In Jewish law, profaning the Temple was among the most serious possible offenses. Paul applies the same seriousness to community-destruction: the factional behavior, the immorality (1 Corinthians 5), the lawsuits (1 Corinthians 6) are all forms of temple-profanation. Ho naos tou Theou hagios estin (God's temple is holy) — holiness as ontological status, not merely a behavioral ideal.
18: Mēdeis heauton exapatatō (do not deceive yourselves): Paul's diagnosis of the Corinthian problem as a form of self-deception. The person who believes they are wise by the standards of this age is epistemically confused: the standards of the present age are passing away (1 Corinthians 7:31), so wisdom by those standards is not genuine wisdom at all. Mōros genesthō hina genētai sophos (become a fool in order to become wise): the paradox echoes the cross-foolishness of 1 Corinthians 1:18 — genuine wisdom requires passing through apparent foolishness first.
19: Paul cites Job 5:13 — catching the wise in their craftiness — and Psalms 94:11 (LXX 1 Corinthians 93:11) — the Lord knows the thoughts of the wise are futile. The Job citation is unusual: Eliphaz is generally not considered a reliable theological voice in Job, but Paul quotes him because the content correctly states the divine perspective. Ancient Jewish interpreters (including the Talmud) sometimes cited Eliphaz approvingly; Paul follows this practice. The Psalms citation grounds the point in Israel's liturgical wisdom tradition.
20: Psalms 94:11 (LXX 1 Corinthians 93:11) in Paul's citation reads ton sophōn (of the wise) where the MT has ha-adam (of humanity/man): Paul uses the LXX variant (or adapts the text) to make it applicable specifically to the wisdom-claiming Corinthians. The dialogismoi (thoughts, reckonings) of the wise are mataioi (futile, empty, vanity) — the LXX vocabulary of Ecclesiastes' hebel (breath, vanity). The divine verdict on human wisdom-projects is that they are ultimately futile.
21: Mēdeis kauchastho en anthrōpois (no boasting about human leaders): the anti-boasting conclusion. Paul follows this with an extraordinary counter-claim: panta gar hymōn estin (all things are yours). The Corinthians have been competing over which apostle to attach themselves to; Paul's response is that they already possess all of them — Paul, Apollos, Peter, the world, life, death, present, future — all belong to the community in Christ. The patron-client framework is completely inverted: they are not clients of the apostles but beneficiaries of a far larger endowment.
22: The catalogue of possessions — Paul, Apollos, Cephas, the world, life, death, present, future — moves from the specific Corinthian factions to cosmic scope. Ho kosmos, zōē, thanatos, enestōta, mellonta: the temporal and cosmic dimensions of existence all belong to the community in Christ. This is an eschatological claim: in Christ, the community already has access to the fullness of creation, though not yet in completed form. The Greco-Roman patron gave clients access to his resources; God in Christ gives the community access to all reality.
23: Hymeis de Christou, Christos de Theou (you are of Christ, and Christ is of God): the climactic theological anchor. The community belongs to Christ, and Christ to God — a relational chain that places the community within the divine life itself. The Corinthian factions claim to belong to Paul, Apollos, or Peter; Paul says they belong to Christ, which means they already possess everything.
